perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed unde r the laws of the State of Michigan, notwithstanding principles of Michigan or any other state, provided Company may in any enforcement action elect to have the law of the forum jurisdiction to apply. The parties irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the jurisdiction in which Company has an office to resolve all issues related to or arising from this Agreement and any business relationship arising from or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of the State of Michigan. The parties irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the jurisdiction in which Company has an office to resolve all issues related to or arising from this Agreement and any business relationship arising from or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'notwithstanding principles of Michigan or any other state, provided Company may in any enforcement action elect to have the law of the forum jurisdiction to apply' restricts the supplier's right to a fair trial by eliminating choice of law provisions beyond Michigan, which can be deemed unfair and against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 445.903(1)(t) prohibits unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce. By limiting the choice of law and forum to Michigan without regard to fairness principles, the agreement could be interpreted as an attempt to deprive the consumer (Supplier) of legal rights in a potentially unfair manner, depending on the specific circumstances and bargaining power.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 331 of 1976 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT (EXCERPT) Act 331 of 1976 445.903 Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in conduct of trade or commerce; rules; applicability of subsection (1)(hh). Sec. 3. (1) Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce are unlawful and are defined as follows: (a) Causing a probability of confusion or misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (b) Using deceptive representations or deceptive designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (c) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have. (d) Representing that goods are new if they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, used, or secondhand. (e) Representing that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, or grade, or that goods are of a particular style or model, if they are of another. (f) Disparaging the goods, services, business, or reputation of another by false or misleading representation of fact. (g) Advertising or representing goods or services with intent not to dispose of those goods or services as advertised or represented. (h) Advertising goods or services with intent not to supply reasonably expectable public demand, unless the advertisement discloses a limitation of quantity in immediate conjunction with the advertised goods or services. (i) Making false or misleading statements of fact concerning the reasons for, existence of, or amounts of price reductions. (j) Representing that a part, replacement, or repair service is needed when it is not. (k) Representing to a party to whom goods
perturbation.justification: The original contract allowed for choice of law provisions beyond Michigan, whereas the changed contract limits the choice of law to only Michigan. The Perturbation Explanation states this restricts the supplier's right to a fair trial and can be against public policy. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 both reference MCL § 445.903(1)(t), which prohibits unfair or unconscionable practices in trade or commerce. By limiting the choice of law to Michigan, the agreement could be interpreted as depriving the consumer of legal rights in an unfair manner. The omission of the clause allowing for other jurisdictions' laws to apply directly contradicts the prohibition of unfair practices as outlined in MCL § 445.903(1)(t).

perturbation.original_text: The interests of Company in the Confidential Information cannot be adequately protected by a remedy at law. This Agreement may be enforced by preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable and legal remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: The interests of Company in the Confidential Information may be protected by a remedy at law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the part about 'preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable and legal remedies' and stating only 'remedy at law' limits the supplier's rights and access to remedies. Confidentiality agreements usually allow for equitable relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 445.1903 explicitly allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. Removing the reference to equitable remedies like preliminary injunctions limits the remedies available to the Company and suggests that only monetary damages are available, which contradicts the availability of injunctive relief under Michigan law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for preliminary injunction and other equitable remedies, while the changed text limits the company to only a remedy at law. MCL § 445.1903(1) states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." This suggests that injunctive relief is explicitly available under Michigan law for misappropriation of trade secrets. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the law by restricting the remedies available, specifically removing the option for injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: All improvements to Company Produc ts, their compone nts, or their unde rlying technology developed during the performance of any purchase order issued by Company to Supplier shall belong to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Improvements to Company Products developed during the performance of any purchase order issued by Company to Supplier shall belong to Company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'their components, or their underlying technology' narrows down the scope of the clause, which can be misconstrued, and might lead to the supplier losing their rights to improvements to their own underlying technology that are created independently from the company.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 445.1902(b) defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. If the supplier develops improvements to its own underlying technology independently, assigning ownership solely to the Company may violate trade secret protections if that technology qualifies as a trade secret. By omitting 'their components, or their underlying technology' the company can lay claim to independently made technology and therefore cause trade secret violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1902 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1902 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1902 Definitions. Sec. 2. As used in this act: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach, or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or any other means. (b) "Misappropriation" means either of the following: (i) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (ii) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who did 1 or more of the following: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means an individual, corporation, partnership, association, governmental entity, or any other legal entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that is both of the following: (i) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (ii) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History: 1998, Act 44
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'their components, or their underlying technology', narrowing the scope of the clause. This omission, according to the law explanation, could allow the company to claim independently developed technology of the supplier, potentially violating trade secret protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically MCL § 445.1902(d), defines a 'trade secret' as information deriving independent economic value from not being generally known and being subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. If the supplier's underlying technology meets this definition and the company misappropriates it, this would contradict the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be enforced by preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable and legal remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be enforced by available legal remedies.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause about 'preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable' remedies limits the supplier's rights and access to remedies. Confidentiality agreements usually allow for equitable relief to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 445.1903(a) specifically provides for injunctive relief to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the reference to 'preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable' remedies, the agreement suggests that injunctive relief is not available, which contradicts Michigan law on trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable remedies', while MCL § 445.1903(a), as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly allows for injunctions to prevent trade secret misappropriation. By omitting the reference to injunctive relief, the modified contract contradicts the law. The original contract provided for 'preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable and legal remedies,' aligning with the legal provision for injunctive relief. The removal of this clause directly contradicts the availability of remedies under Michigan law, particularly concerning trade secrets.
